questionText: A velocity selector having uniform perpendicular electric and magnetic fields is shown in Figure 20.57. The electric field is provided by a $150\mathrm{V}$ dc battery connected across two large parallel metal plates that are $4.50\mathrm{cm}$ apart. (a) What must be the magnitude of the magnetic field so that charges having a velocity of $3.25\mathrm{km/s}$ perpendicular to the fields will pass through undeflected? (b) Show how the magnetic field should point in the region between the plates.
expectedAnswer: a) 1.02 T  b) E is upward, B is out of the page

parts.label: a
parts.question: What must be the magnitude of the magnetic field so that charges having a velocity of $3.25\mathrm{km/s}$ perpendicular to the fields will pass through undeflected?

parts.label: b
parts.question: Show how the magnetic field should point in the region between the plates.

questionText: An electron moves at $2.50 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~m/s}$ through a region in which there is a magnetic field of unspecified direction and magnitude $7.40 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{T}$. (a) What are the largest and smallest possible magnitudes of the force on the electron due to the magnetic field? (b) If the force on the electron is half of its maximum value, what is the angle between the electron's velocity and the magnetic field?

parts.label: a
parts.question: What are the largest and smallest possible magnitudes of the force on the electron due to the magnetic field?

parts.label: b
parts.question: If the force on the electron is half of its maximum value, what is the angle between the electron's velocity and the magnetic field?

questionText: In a cloud chamber experiment, a proton enters a uniform 0.250 T magnetic field directed perpendicular to its motion. You measure the proton's path on a photograph and find that it follows a circular arc of radius 6.13 cm. How fast was the proton moving?

An alpha particle (a He nucleus, containing two protons and two neutrons and having a mass of $6.64 \times 10^{-27} \mathrm{~kg}$) traveling horizontally at $35.6 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{s}$ enters a uniform, vertical, $1.10 \mathrm{T}$ magnetic field. (a) What is the diameter of the path followed by this alpha particle? (b) What effect does the magnetic field have on the speed of the particle? (c) What are the magnitude and direction of the acceleration of the alpha particle while it is in the magnetic field? (d) Explain why the speed of the particle does not change even though an unbalanced external force acts on it.
expectedAnswer: a) 0.673 mm  b) 0 J (speed is constant)  c) 1.88 x 10^12 m/s^2  d) speed is constant

parts.label: a
parts.question: What is the diameter of the path followed by this alpha particle?

parts.label: b
parts.question: What effect does the magnetic field have on the speed of the particle?

parts.label: c
parts.question: What are the magnitude and direction of the acceleration of the alpha particle while it is in the magnetic field?

parts.label: d
parts.question: Explain why the speed of the particle does not change even though an unbalanced external force acts on it.

questionText: A deuteron particle (the nucleus of an isotope of hydrogen consisting of one proton and one neutron and having a mass of $3.34 \times 10^{-27} \mathrm{~kg}$) moving horizontally enters a uniform, vertical, $0.500 \mathrm{T}$ magnetic field and follows a circular arc of radius $55.6 \mathrm{cm}$. (a) How fast was this deuteron moving just before it entered the magnetic field and just after it came out of the field? (b) What would be the radius of the arc followed by a proton that entered the field with the same velocity as the deuteron?

parts.label: a
parts.question: How fast was this deuteron moving just before it entered the magnetic field and just after it came out of the field?

parts.label: b
parts.question: What would be the radius of the arc followed by a proton that entered the field with the same velocity as the deuteron?

questionText: A beam of protons traveling at $1.20\mathrm{km / s}$ enters a uniform magnetic field, traveling perpendicular to the field. The beam exits the magnetic field in a direction perpendicular to its original direction (Figure 20.58). The beam travels a distance of $1.18\mathrm{cm}$ while in the field. What is the magnitude of the magnetic field?
A uniform magnetic field bends an electron in a circular arc of radius $R$. What will be the radius of the arc (in terms of $R$) if the field is tripled?

An electron at point $A$ in Figure 20.59 has a speed $v_{0}$ of $1.41 \times 10^{6} \, \mathrm{m/s}$. Find (a) the magnitude and direction of the magnetic field that will cause the electron to follow the semicircular path from $A$ to $B$ and (b) the time required for the electron to move from $A$ to $B$. (c) What magnetic field would be needed if the particle were a proton instead of an electron?
expectedAnswer: a) 1.61 x 10^-4 T, into the page  b) 1.11 x 10^-7 s  c) 0.294 T, out of the page

parts.label: a
parts.question: the magnitude and direction of the magnetic field that will cause the electron to follow the semicircular path from $A$ to $B$

parts.label: b
parts.question: the time required for the electron to move from $A$ to $B$.

parts.label: c
parts.question: What magnetic field would be needed if the particle were a proton instead of an electron?

questionText: A beam of protons is accelerated through a potential difference of $0.745\mathrm{kV}$ and then enters a uniform magnetic field traveling perpendicular to the field. (a) What magnitude of field is needed to bend these protons in a circular arc of diameter $1.75\mathrm{m}$? (b) What magnetic field would be needed to produce a path with the same diameter if the particles were electrons having the same speed as the protons?

parts.label: a
parts.question: What magnitude of field is needed to bend these protons in a circular arc of diameter $1.75\mathrm{m}$?

parts.label: b
parts.question: What magnetic field would be needed to produce a path with the same diameter if the particles were electrons having the same speed as the protons?

questionText: A $3.25\mathrm{g}$ bullet picks up an electric charge of $1.65\mu \mathrm{C}$ as it travels down the barrel of a rifle. It leaves the barrel at a speed of $425~\mathrm{m / s}$, traveling perpendicular to the earth's magnetic field, which has a magnitude of $5.50\times 10^{-4}\mathrm{T}$. Calculate (a) the magnitude of the magnetic force on the bullet and (b) the magnitude of the bullet's acceleration due to the magnetic force at the instant it leaves the rifle barrel.

parts.label: a
parts.question: the magnitude of the magnetic force on the bullet

parts.label: b
parts.question: the magnitude of the bullet's acceleration due to the magnetic force at the instant it leaves the rifle barrel.

questionText: An electron travels into a $0.3\mathrm{T}$ magnetic field perpendicular to its path, where it moves in a circular arc of diameter $0.020\mathrm{m}$. What is the kinetic energy of the electron?

(a) What is the speed of a beam of electrons when the simultaneous influence of an electric field of $1.56 \times 10^{4} \mathrm{~V/m}$ and a magnetic field of $4.62 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{T}$, with both fields normal to the beam and to each other, produces no deflection of the electrons? (b) In a diagram, show the relative orientations of the vectors $\vec{v}$, $\vec{E}$, and $\vec{B}$. (c) When the electric field is removed, what is the radius of the electron orbit? What is the period of the orbit?
expectedAnswer: a) 3.38 \times 10^6 m/s  b) See figure  c) R = 4.17 \times 10^{-3} m, T = 7.74 \times 10^{-9} s

parts.label: a
parts.question: What is the speed of a beam of electrons when the simultaneous influence of an electric field of $1.56 \times 10^{4} \mathrm{~V/m}$ and a magnetic field of $4.62 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{T}$, with both fields normal to the beam and to each other, produces no deflection of the electrons?

parts.label: b
parts.question: In a diagram, show the relative orientations of the vectors $\vec{v}$, $\vec{E}$, and $\vec{B}$.

parts.label: c
parts.question: When the electric field is removed, what is the radius of the electron orbit? What is the period of the orbit?

questionText: Singly ionized (one electron removed) atoms are accelerated and then passed through a velocity selector consisting of perpendicular electric and magnetic fields. The electric field is $155\mathrm{V / m}$ and the magnetic field is $0.0315\mathrm{T}$. The ions next enter a uniform magnetic field of magnitude $0.0175\mathrm{T}$ that is oriented perpendicular to their velocity. (a) How fast are the ions moving when they emerge from the velocity selector?

parts.label: a
parts.question: How fast are the ions moving when they emerge from the velocity selector?

questionText: BIO Ancient meat eating. The amount of meat in prehistoric diets can be determined by measuring the ratio of the isotopes nitrogen-15 to nitrogen-14 in bone from human remains. Carnivores concentrate $^{15}\mathrm{N}$, so this ratio tells archaeologists how much meat was consumed by ancient people. Suppose you use a velocity selector to obtain singly ionized (missing one electron) nitrogen atoms of speed $8.50\mathrm{km / s}$ and bend them along a semicircle within a uniform magnetic field. If the $^{14}\mathrm{N}$ atoms travel along a semicircle with a diameter of $29.2\mathrm{cm}$, find the separation of the $^{14}\mathrm{N}$ and $^{15}\mathrm{N}$ isotopes at the detector. The measured masses of these isotopes are $2.32\times 10^{-26}\mathrm{kg}$ $(^{14}\mathrm{N})$ and $2.49\times 10^{-26}\mathrm{kg}$ $(^{15}\mathrm{N})$.

A straight vertical wire carries a current of $1.20\mathrm{A}$ downward in a region between the poles of a large electromagnet where the field strength is $0.588\mathrm{T}$ and is horizontal. What are the magnitude and direction of the magnetic force on a $1.00\mathrm{cm}$ section of this wire if the magnetic-field direction is (a) toward the east, (b) toward the south, (c) $30.0^{\circ}$ south of west?
expectedAnswer: a) 7.06 \times 10^{-3} N, south  b) 7.06 \times 10^{-3} N, west  c) 7.06 \times 10^{-3} N, 60.0^{\circ} north of west

parts.label: a
parts.question: toward the east

parts.label: b
parts.question: toward the south

parts.label: c
parts.question: $30.0^{\circ}$ south of west?

questionText: Magnetic force on a lightning bolt. Currents during lightning strikes can be up to 50,000 A (or more!). We can model such a strike as a 50,000 A vertical current perpendicular to the earth's magnetic field, which is about $\frac{1}{2}$ gauss. What is the force on each meter of this current due to the earth's magnetic field?

A horizontal rod $0.200\mathrm{m}$ long carries a current through a uniform horizontal magnetic field of magnitude $0.067\mathrm{T}$ that points perpendicular to the rod. If the magnetic force on this rod is measured to be $0.13\mathrm{N}$, what is the current flowing through the rod?

A straight $2.5\mathrm{m}$ wire carries a typical household current of $1.5\mathrm{A}$ (in one direction) at a location where the earth's magnetic field is 0.55 gauss from south to north. Find the magnitude and direction of the force that our planet's magnetic field exerts on this wire if it is oriented so that the current in it is running (a) from west to east, (b) vertically upward, (c) from north to south. (d) Is the magnetic force ever large enough to cause significant effects under normal household conditions?
expectedAnswer: a) 2.1 \times 10^{-4} N, upward  b) 2.1 \times 10^{-4} N, east to west  c) 0  d) Not large enough to cause significant effects

parts.label: a
parts.question: from west to east

parts.label: b
parts.question: vertically upward

parts.label: c
parts.question: from north to south

parts.label: d
parts.question: Is the magnetic force ever large enough to cause significant effects under normal household conditions?

questionText: A magnetic field is used to suspend a wire of mass $0.02\mathrm{kg}$ and length $0.15\mathrm{m}$. The wire is carrying a current of $10\mathrm{A}$. What minimum magnetic-field magnitude is needed to balance the pull of gravity?

A rectangular $10.0\mathrm{cm}$ by $20.0\mathrm{cm}$ circuit carrying an 8.00 A current is oriented with its plane parallel to a uniform 0.750 T magnetic field (Figure 20.60). (a) Find the magnitude and direction of the magnetic force on each segment (ab, bc, cd, and da) of this circuit. Illustrate your answers with clear diagrams. (b) Find the magnitude of the net force on the entire circuit.
expectedAnswer: a) da: 0; ab: 1.2 N into page; bc: 0; cd: 1.2 N out of page  b) 0

parts.label: a
parts.question: Find the magnitude and direction of the magnetic force on each segment (ab, bc, cd, and da) of this circuit. Illustrate your answers with clear diagrams.

parts.label: b
parts.question: Find the magnitude of the net force on the entire circuit.

questionText: A long wire carrying a 6.00 A current reverses direction by means of two right-angle bends, as shown in Figure 20.61. The part of the wire where the bend occurs is in a magnetic field of 0.666 T confined to the circular region of diameter 75 cm, as shown. Find the magnitude and direction of the net force that the magnetic field exerts on this wire.

A long wire carrying 4.50 A of current makes two $90^{\circ}$ bends, as shown in Figure 20.62. The bent part of the wire passes through a uniform 0.240 T magnetic field directed as shown in the figure and confined to a limited region of space. Find the magnitude and direction of the force that the magnetic field exerts on the wire.

The $20.0\mathrm{cm}$ by $35.0\mathrm{cm}$ rectangular circuit shown in Figure 20.63 is hinged along side $ab$. It carries a clockwise $5.00\mathrm{A}$ current and is located in a uniform $1.20\mathrm{T}$ magnetic field oriented perpendicular to two of its sides, as shown. (a) Make a clear diagram showing the direction of the force that the magnetic field exerts on each segment of the circuit (ab, bc, cd, and da). (b) Of the four forces you drew in part (a), decide which ones exert a torque about the hinge ab. Then calculate only those forces that exert this torque. (c) Use your results from part (b) to calculate the torque that the magnetic field exerts on the circuit about the hinge axis $ab$.
expectedAnswer: a) See figure  b) F_{cd} = 1.20 N

parts.label: a
parts.question: Make a clear diagram showing the direction of the force that the magnetic field exerts on each segment of the circuit (ab, bc, cd, and da).

parts.label: b
parts.question: Of the four forces you drew in part (a), decide which ones exert a torque about the hinge ab. Then calculate only those forces that exert this torque.

parts.label: c
parts.question: Use your results from part (b) to calculate the torque that the magnetic field exerts on the circuit about the hinge axis $ab$.

questionText: The plane of a $5.0\mathrm{cm}$ by $8.0\mathrm{cm}$ rectangular loop of wire is parallel to a $0.19\mathrm{T}$ magnetic field, and the loop carries a current of $6.2\mathrm{A}$. (a) What torque acts on the loop? (b) What is the magnetic moment of the loop?

parts.label: a
parts.question: What torque acts on the loop?

parts.label: b
parts.question: What is the magnetic moment of the loop?

questionText: A circular coil of wire $8.6\mathrm{cm}$ in diameter has 15 turns and carries a current of $2.7\mathrm{A}$. The coil is in a region where the magnetic field is $0.56\mathrm{T}$. (a) What orientation of the coil gives the maximum torque on the coil, and what is this maximum torque? (b) For what orientation of the coil is the magnitude of the torque $71\%$ of the maximum found in part (a)?

parts.label: a
parts.question: What orientation of the coil gives the maximum torque on the coil, and what is this maximum torque?

parts.label: b
parts.question: For what orientation of the coil is the magnitude of the torque $71\%$ of the maximum found in part (a)?
